costs: Administrative capacity needed to conduct segregation-impact assessments consistently and defensibly
Potential legal challenge from church/private maintainers regarding the extension of discretionary refusal to their sector
Risk of politically sensitive conflict with religious/private institutional stakeholders
content.equity_impact: Provides a maintainer-neutral backstop against further segregation growth from track expansion, complementing state-sector contraction (T2) and admissions redesign (T1) by closing the church/private expansion loophole; does not reduce currently existing segregation levels but caps future growth in the segregation-generating channel most likely to escape other levers.
expected_benefits: Closes a documented gap where state-sector-only levers (T2) cannot reach church/private track expansion
Builds directly on an already-exercised, legally tested precedent, minimizing novel legal risk relative to structural reform (T3)
Prevents future segregation growth even if it does not reduce existing track size
content.goal: Use and formalize existing ministerial discretionary approval power to prevent new or expanded 6/8-year track approvals that would foreseeably increase local segregation, closing the gap left by state-sector-only contraction levers.

implementation_steps.action: Codify a standardized segregation-impact assessment (modeled on the Tiszavasvári precedent) that all new/expanded 6/8-year track applications must pass, applicable to all maintainer types
Confirm legal basis for extending discretionary refusal power over church/foundation/private applicants under the 2023 unified legal-status framework
Apply the segregation-impact test to all pending and future track-expansion applications
implementation_steps.actor: Klebelsberg Centre and relevant church/private oversight bodies

implementation_steps.action: Publish annual transparency report on approvals/denials and their segregation-impact assessments to build public trust and precedent consistency

mechanisms: The Encs case shows that permission for a school to add lower grades (7-8) directly caused creaming of higher-status pupils from feeder primary schools, establishing a documented causal channel from expansion approval to local segregation (F-live-hungarian_education_system-07).

The ministry has already exercised discretionary refusal in the Tiszavasvári case explicitly citing segregation risk, establishing a legal/administrative precedent that a formalized test would generalize and make consistent (F-live-hungarian_education_system-09).

A 2023 law extended uniform state employment/legal-status terms across all provider types including church and private, indicating an existing legal channel through which cross-maintainer regulatory reach has already been expanded and could be extended to approval-gatekeeping (F-live-legal_and_governance-09).

Church-run institutions have grown substantially (8.6% to 16.6% of primary, 10% to 26.1% of secondary schools 2010-2021) with documented admissions discretion enabling selection of higher-achieving students, indicating the segregation-expansion risk is concentrated precisely in the maintainer segment least covered by existing state-sector capacity levers like T2 (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-07, F-live-implementation_planning-05).
risks: Church and private maintainers may contest the extension of ministerial discretion over their admissions/expansion decisions as an infringement on institutional autonomy, especially given their partial exemption from state maintenance jurisdiction (F-live-legal_and_governance-07)
A purely preventive (expansion-blocking) tool does not reduce existing segregation from already-approved tracks, limiting its scope to marginal future growth only
Inconsistent or politically selective application of the segregation-impact test could undermine its legitimacy and invite accusations of arbitrary discretion
content.title: Targeted administrative constraint on segregation-generating track expansion (discretionary gatekeeping across all maintainer types)
